paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 29: The Sudden Storm (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 3 girl who, when a thunderstorm breaks out one afternoon, rushes to bring in the laundry her elderly neighbour Auntie Goh has hung along their HDB corridor, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [10 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format, pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 7 short-answer items (Q1–Q7) and a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q8) are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The afternoon had started bright and lazy. I was lying on the sofa with a Geography textbook open on my chest, not really reading it, while the ceiling fan turned slowly above me. Outside our flat on the eighth floor, the Tampines sky was a flat, friendly blue. My parents were both at work, and the whole quiet afternoon belonged to me.

Paragraph 2
Then the light changed. Without any warning the blue drained out of the sky, and a grey shadow slid across our living room. I sat up. From the corridor came a low, rolling sound, far away at first, then closer — thunder. A cool wind pushed through the window and lifted the edge of my textbook. I knew that wind. In a few minutes the rain would come down hard.

Paragraph 3
That was when I remembered Auntie Goh. She lived two doors down, alone, and every morning she hung her washing on the bamboo poles along our shared corridor — blouses, a thin cotton blanket, three or four faded handkerchiefs. She was almost eighty and walked with a stick. She had gone to her daughter's place in Yishun that morning; I had seen her wait for the lift with her umbrella. Her laundry was still out there, and the rain was coming.

Paragraph 4
I did not stop to think. I pushed my feet into my slippers and ran out of the flat. The first fat drops were already hitting the corridor floor, leaving dark coins on the concrete. Auntie Goh's washing flapped wildly on the poles, the cotton blanket snapping like a sail. I reached up and began pulling the clothes down, piling them over my arm as fast as I could.

Paragraph 5
The poles were heavier and higher than I expected. I am not tall, and I had to stand on the tips of my toes, stretching until my shoulder ached. The wind kept tugging the blanket out of my hands. Rain blew sideways into my face and soaked one whole sleeve of my T-shirt. A handkerchief escaped and sailed over the railing, and I watched it spin down eight floors into the grey. I could not save that one. But I saved the rest.

Paragraph 6
I carried the bundle of washing back into my own flat just as the storm broke open completely. Through the window the rain came down in straight silver rods, and the world below disappeared. I laid Auntie Goh's clothes carefully over the backs of our dining chairs, smoothing out the blanket. Only the very corners were damp. I had been in time.

Paragraph 7
When Auntie Goh came home that evening, I knocked on her door and gave everything back, folded. She held the dry blanket against her cheek and was quiet for a moment. 'You stood in the rain for an old woman's washing,' she said at last. I told her about the handkerchief that had flown away, and that I was sorry. She only laughed. 'One handkerchief,' she said. 'You think I would trade one handkerchief for a neighbour like you?'

Paragraph 8
The next morning a small plastic bag was hanging on our door handle: four warm curry puffs from the shop downstairs, and a brand-new packet of handkerchiefs. I never told my parents the whole story. But after that, whenever the sky began to turn grey in the afternoon, I found myself glancing down the corridor first — to see whose washing was still out, and whether anyone was home to bring it in.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past exam. HDB: Housing and Development Board public-housing flats; corridor: the shared open walkway outside the flats on each floor; bamboo poles: long poles pushed out through holders to hang washing to dry; curry puff: a small fried pastry with a savoury filling.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what details show that the afternoon felt slow and relaxed before the storm? [1]
questions.answer: the narrator was lying on the sofa not really reading her Geography textbook, the ceiling fan was turning slowly above her, the sky was a flat friendly blue, and the whole quiet afternoon belonged to her with her parents away at work

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 2, how did the narrator know that heavy rain was coming even before it started? [1]
questions.answer: the blue drained out of the sky and a grey shadow slid across the room, she heard thunder rolling closer, and a cool wind pushed through the window — she recognised that wind and knew from experience that in a few minutes the rain would come down hard

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 3, why was the narrator worried about Auntie Goh's laundry in particular? [1]
questions.answer: Auntie Goh was almost eighty, lived alone and walked with a stick, and she had gone to her daughter's place in Yishun that morning, so she was not home to bring in her washing herself before the rain arrived

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the phrase 'leaving dark coins on the concrete' to describe the first raindrops (Paragraph 4)? [1]
questions.answer: it is a vivid image comparing the round wet marks of the first fat drops to coins scattered on the floor, helping the reader picture exactly how the rain looked as it began and creating a sense that time was running out

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 5, what details show that bringing in the washing was difficult for the narrator? [1]
questions.answer: the poles were heavier and higher than she expected and she is not tall, so she had to stand on the tips of her toes and stretch until her shoulder ached, the wind kept tugging the blanket out of her hands, and rain blew sideways and soaked one whole sleeve of her T-shirt

questions.stem: Q6. What does Auntie Goh mean in Paragraph 7 when she says, 'You think I would trade one handkerchief for a neighbour like you?' [1]
questions.answer: she means that the kindness the narrator showed is worth far more than a single lost handkerchief — she values having such a thoughtful, caring neighbour much more than the small thing that was lost, so the narrator should not feel sorry

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the closing detail that the narrator now glances down the corridor first whenever the sky turns grey (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it shows that the experience has changed her — she has become more aware of and responsible for her neighbours, automatically thinking of others' washing and whether anyone is home to help, which reflects the lasting neighbourly care she has learnt

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the afternoon's storm unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: peaceful / alarmed / determined / touched / jealous / impatient / fearful / indifferent. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'The Sudden Storm' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the afternoon unfolds — from peaceful in Paragraph 1 as she lies lazily on the sofa under the slow fan with the quiet afternoon to herself, to alarmed in Paragraphs 2 and 3 when the sky suddenly darkens, thunder rolls in and she realises Auntie Goh's washing is still out in the coming rain, to determined in Paragraphs 4 and 5 as she runs out and struggles on tiptoe against the wind and rain to pull the clothes down in time, ending touched in Paragraph 7 when Auntie Goh holds the dry blanket to her cheek and says she would not trade one handkerchief for a neighbour like her. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 1 as she lies on the sofa with her Geography textbook open on her chest, the ceiling fan turning slowly, the sky a flat friendly blue, and the whole quiet afternoon belonging to her? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 2 and 3 when the sky suddenly drains to grey, thunder rolls closer, a cool wind warns of hard rain, and she remembers that elderly Auntie Goh's washing is still out on the corridor poles? [1]

Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4 and 5 as she runs out into the first rain, stands on tiptoe with her shoulder aching, and keeps pulling the clothes down even as the wind tugs the blanket away and rain soaks her sleeve? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7 when Auntie Goh holds the dry blanket against her cheek, falls quiet, and tells her she would never trade one handkerchief for a neighbour like her? [1]
